company.name: Broadcom

marketCap.rationale: Computed from Avago's $15.00 IPO price and 235,888,203 shares outstanding immediately after the offering in the final prospectus.

company.description: Semiconductor and infrastructure software consolidator with critical exposure to networking and virtualization.

overview.title: Where chips meet enterprise rent extraction
paragraphs: Broadcom is an awkwardly elegant example of two different moat types living under one roof: hard-to-replicate semiconductor supply chains and highly monetizable enterprise software lock-in.
That split makes Broadcom less decentralizable than software vendors but more vulnerable than a pure semiconductor titan in specific categories like VMware-era virtualization.

moatNarrative: Broadcom's moat comes from custom silicon relationships, networking relevance, and a willingness to monetize mission-critical software with all the tenderness of a spreadsheet.
Customers may dislike the pricing; they also tend to dislike downtime, which is usually what preserves the margin.
decentralizationNarrative: The software side is replaceable in pockets. The semiconductor side is much less so.
That makes Broadcom a mixed case: some value capture looks structurally durable, while some looks like a generous invitation for Proxmox and friends.
company.snapshotNote: Curated early-2026 public-source snapshot. Exact ordering can drift with the tape.

moat.rationale: Broadcom combines hard-to-replicate hardware supply with entrenched enterprise software footprints.

decentralizability.rationale: Virtualization can be displaced more readily than Broadcom's semiconductor relevance.

profitability.rationale: The company has both pricing power and critical infrastructure exposure.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate valuation snapshot reflecting AI and infrastructure enthusiasm.

marketCap.rationale: Approximate market cap snapshot from public market trackers.

products.name: VMware virtualization stack
products.summary: Virtualization and private cloud control plane software.
products.whyItMatters: This is one of Broadcom's most obviously replaceable rent-heavy layers.
replacementSketch: Open virtualization stacks already exist and are increasingly production-worthy.
Incumbent pricing pressure often does half the adoption work for the alternatives.

alternatives.name: Proxmox VE
alternatives.summary: Open-source virtualization stack with increasing enterprise appeal.

readiness.rationale: Now credible for a wide range of production workloads.

costLeverage.rationale: Directly compresses premium VMware pricing logic.

products.name: Broadcom networking silicon
products.summary: Critical chips and connectivity layers for data-center and telecom infrastructure.
products.whyItMatters: This is the part of Broadcom that remains stubbornly physical, capital-intensive, and supply-chain bound.
replacementSketch: Open networking software matters, but replacing the silicon layer itself is much slower and more capital-intensive.
Localized electronics tooling may erode some hardware rents over time, but not overnight and not at frontier scale.

alternatives.name: Open networking software stacks
alternatives.summary: Open switching and control plane efforts that erode some software-side networking lock-in.

openness.rationale: Moves some value away from closed control surfaces.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Helpful, but the silicon supply chain remains stubbornly concentrated.

readiness.rationale: Useful in pockets, still far from replacing the entire incumbent stack.

costLeverage.rationale: Reduces some premium capture without overturning the hardware layer.
